title: The labour market
summary:
## Applying demand & supply to labour

The **labour market** applies demand and supply to a **factor** (labour), setting the **wage rate** and level of employment. Labour demand is a **derived demand** — firms want workers for the output they produce, not for their own sake.

## Demand for labour

The demand for labour comes from the **marginal revenue product (MRP)**:
> **MRP = marginal physical product × marginal revenue (≈ price of the output).**
A profit-maximising firm hires up to where **MRP = wage rate**. MRP eventually falls (diminishing returns), giving a downward-sloping labour-demand curve.

**Determinants / elasticity of labour demand:** productivity, the price of the product, the proportion of labour in total costs, and the ease of substituting capital for labour. Demand is more elastic when labour is a big share of costs and easily substituted.

## Supply of labour

The supply of labour to an **occupation** depends on the wage relative to alternatives, plus **non-monetary factors** (job satisfaction, conditions, qualifications required, training time). For an individual, higher wages usually raise hours worked, but a **backward-bending supply curve** can occur when the **income effect** (choosing more leisure) outweighs the **substitution effect** at high wages.

**Wage differentials** (why wages differ between jobs) arise from differences in MRP/skill, qualifications and training, labour (im)mobility, non-monetary characteristics (**compensating differentials**), and discrimination.

## Wage determination

In a **competitive labour market**, the wage and employment level are set where **labour demand (MRP) = labour supply**. Firms are **wage takers** facing a horizontal supply of labour at the market wage.

## Imperfect labour markets

- **Monopsony** — a **single (dominant) buyer of labour** (e.g. a large employer, or the government for nurses). A monopsonist restricts employment and pays a **lower wage** than a competitive market (marginal cost of labour lies above the supply curve).
- **Trade unions** — collective bargaining raises wages above the market level; in a competitive market this can **reduce employment**, but in a **monopsony** a union (bilateral monopoly) can raise **both wages and employment** toward the competitive level.
- **Minimum wage** — a wage floor: protects low-paid workers and can boost demand/motivation, but may cause unemployment if set above the equilibrium (though in a monopsony it can raise employment).

## Why it matters

The labour market links micro theory (MRP, market structures) to real-world issues of wages, inequality and unemployment, and it underpins government intervention on minimum wages and union law (Unit 8). Expect diagram-based analysis of monopsony, unions and minimum wages.

keyTerms.definition: Demand for a factor that arises from demand for the goods it helps produce.
The extra revenue from employing one more worker: marginal product × marginal revenue.
A difference in wages between jobs or workers, due to skill, MRP, mobility or non-monetary factors.
Extra pay to offset unattractive job characteristics (e.g. danger or unsocial hours).
When higher wages eventually reduce hours worked as the income effect outweighs the substitution effect.
A market with a single or dominant buyer of labour, which lowers wages and employment.
An organisation of workers that bargains collectively to raise wages and improve conditions.
A legal wage floor to protect low-paid workers.

diagrams: **Competitive labour market:** downward MRP (labour demand) and upward labour supply; equilibrium wage and employment at their intersection.
**Monopsony:** supply of labour (= average cost of labour) with the marginal cost of labour above it; employment set where MCL = MRP, wage read off the supply curve → lower wage and employment.
**Trade union / minimum wage:** show a wage set above equilibrium and the resulting excess supply (unemployment) in a competitive market, or the employment-raising effect in a monopsony.
**Backward-bending supply curve:** individual labour supply rising then bending back at high wages.
tips: Define labour demand as **derived demand** driven by **MRP**, and state the hiring rule **MRP = wage**.
Draw and explain the **monopsony diagram** (MCL above supply → lower wage and employment) — a favourite exam topic.
Show the **union/minimum-wage effect differs** between a competitive market (possible unemployment) and a monopsony (can raise employment) — strong evaluation.
Use **wage differentials** (MRP, skills, mobility, compensating differentials, discrimination) to explain pay gaps.
Introduce the **backward-bending supply curve** via income vs substitution effects for A2-level depth.

channels.why: Clear labour-market, MRP and monopsony coverage.

keyConcepts.title: Trade unions and the minimum wage
